Were You Asked This?
Design an Anti-Phishing System

Design an anti-phishing backend system for Chrome that intercepts navigation to malicious websites and shows users a warning page, while still allowing them to proceed if they choose. The system should handle daily updates of the complete malicious URL list from internal security analysts.

Asked at:

Google

Chrome’s anti-phishing system is a safety service that checks URLs as users navigate, warns when a destination is suspected to be malicious, and lets users proceed at their own risk. It relies on a backend that publishes a daily, signed list of malicious URLs and patterns, which clients download and use for very fast, privacy-preserving checks.

Interviewers ask this to assess your ability to design client-centric safety features with extremely low latency, high reliability, and privacy constraints at global scale. It tests your judgment about client vs server trade-offs, large update distribution, canonicalization to avoid evasion, and how to handle contention from billions of devices updating on a schedule. Expect to discuss data representation, rollout/versioning, and failure modes.

Community Solutions
Design an Anti-Phishing System
by StraightforwardAquamarineVulture342
1
3
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to see an interstitial warning page when navigating to a known malicious URL.

Users should be able to proceed to the site after seeing the warning (with clear risk messaging).

Users should be able to benefit from daily updates of the full malicious URL list applied automatically in the background.

Users should be able to browse with low latency and strong privacy, including when offline or on poor networks.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you achieve sub-10ms URL checks on navigation while preserving user privacy?

How will you distribute the full daily list to hundreds of millions of Chrome clients without a thundering herd or CDN overload?

How will you canonicalize and match URLs to avoid evasion (punycode, subdomains, paths, query strings) while minimizing false positives?

What are your failure modes and safety policies (fail-open vs fail-closed) during outages or when the list is stale?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Dealing with Contention

Publishing a full list once per day to a massive client fleet can create a synchronized request storm. You must spread load with randomized schedules, strong CDN caching, resumable downloads, and staged rollouts to avoid self-inflicted outages.

Read More
Scaling Reads

The hot path is read-heavy: every navigation triggers a check. Designs rely on client-side indexed data and CDN-served static bundles to keep read latency well under user-perceptible thresholds, with rare server lookups for verification.

Read More
Handling Large Blobs

The malicious list can be large and grows over time. Chunked packaging, compression, content-addressing, and efficient storage on device are essential to make daily full-list updates practical and reliable across diverse networks.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

A server-side verification service can use Redis as an in-memory store of full URL hashes for sub-millisecond membership checks during the second-phase lookup, enabling high QPS with predictable latency.

Read More
Cassandra

For durable storage of the canonical malicious entries, historical versions, and rollout metadata, Cassandra provides scalable, multi-region writes and reads with tunable consistency—useful for building and serving daily list artifacts.

Read More
API Gateway

An API Gateway can front the verification and manifest endpoints, providing global routing, rate limiting, authentication of clients, and canary/staged deployments without exposing backend services directly.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Web Crawler

Both require rigorous URL canonicalization, deduplication, and handling of edge cases (punycode, redirects, query parameters). The scale of URL handling and normalization strategies closely parallels crawler design concerns.

Read
Practice
Bitly

A URL shortener must validate and sometimes safety-check target URLs on click, emphasizing fast redirect paths, low latency, and safe browsing checks—similar to interstitial warnings and fast membership lookups.

Read
Practice
Distributed Cache

Clients effectively maintain a local cache of a large global dataset with periodic refresh. Concepts like cache invalidation, versioning, and consistency windows map well to distributing the daily malicious list.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Relying solely on server-side lookups for every navigation

This introduces unacceptable latency, harms privacy by leaking browsing history, and creates a scalability bottleneck. Chrome needs near-instant, offline-capable checks; server-only designs will not meet user experience or reliability goals.

Ignoring URL canonicalization and internationalization nuances

Without consistent normalization (punycode, eTLD+1, path/query handling), attackers can easily evade detection or you’ll trigger false positives. This undermines security and user trust in the warnings.

Not smoothing daily updates (thundering herd)

Publishing a new full list and letting all clients fetch immediately can overwhelm origins and CDNs, causing outages and failed updates. Lack of jitter, caching, and staged rollout is a major reliability flaw.

Desing and anti-physing backend system that stops users from navigating to malicious websites. The user will see a warning instead of the website and they can still navigate to the URL if they want to. Assume that we are inside google an this only needs to work for chrome. Also the list of malicious websites is updated once a day by a team of internal analysts. The full list of URLs is published, not only changes.

V
VoicelessTealMongoose648
Premium
• 5个月前

Default to local checks even if the network is down; when the remote verification service is unavailable, show a softer warning or proceed with caution rather than hard-blocking.

Enforce max staleness on the local list (e.g., warn if older than X days) and include an emergency blocklist/allowlist channel for zero-day outbreaks and false positives.
Keep interstitial UX consistent and auditable: log anonymized metrics, version-tag warnings, and provide admin/enterprise policies to override behavior where required.

A fan of the way the whole hello interview question breakdowns, patterns, key techs are written but statements like above are spread across all Real interview question solutions and similar responses are provided by the "Ask Tutor" functionality as well. While these statements try to be complete in nature, they are very terse and end up confusing and scaring more than needed :). Would appreciate more, even if the response touches only 2 pieces instead of 5 but provides more details like "Sign the manifest file using private-public key < more details> and use staged rollout <more details>" instead of writing every possible solution that can fit for that item. Just a suggestion!
